elements.sent-2411-123: In this work, we theoretically analyze the phase diagrams of a unique heavy-fermion material CeCo$_2$P$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Key results include: (1) the quantum geometry and relatively narrow bands of Co $d$ orbitals stabilize a type-A AFM phase; (2) the $P\cdot\mathcal{T}$ symmetry and the filling-enforced metallic $c$ electrons offer a robust mechanism that supports the development of the Kondo phase, even within the magnetically ordered phase; (3) the composite $f$-electron excitations in the Kondo phase exhibit non-trivial band topology characterized by a glide-mirror-$z$-protected nodal line.

elements.section-2411-010: Kondo effect in the AFM phase
elements.sent-2411-360: In this section, we demonstrate even with AFM ordering of $c$ electrons a Kondo effect can still develop.
elements.sent-2411-361: Due to the existence of $P\cdot\mathcal{T}$ symmetry in the AFM phase (where $P$ denotes inversion symmetry and $\mathcal{T}$ denotes time-reversal symmetry), $c$ electrons can still form two-fold degenerate Kramers' doublets.
elements.sent-2411-362: The doublets of $c$ electrons can then screen the $f$-local moments.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{fig/1d_kondo.png} \caption{Illustration of the Kondo effect in the AFM phase. Red arrows denote the spins of $c$ electrons, and blue arrows denote the spins of $f$ electrons. The ground state is a Kondo-singlet state (Eq.~\ref{eq:toy_model_kondo_singlet}). } \label{fig:1d_non_local_kondo}
elements.sent-2411-363: We first illustrate the formation of the Kondo singlet in the AFM phase via a three-site spinful toy model.
elements.sent-2411-364: The Hamiltonian of the toy model is \ba H = \sum_{i=1,2,\sigma (-\mu) c_{i,\sigma ^\dag c_{i,\sigma +\sum_{\sigma_1,\sigma_2 m_z (c_{1,\sigma_1 ^\dag \sigma^z_{\sigma_1\sigma_2 c_{1,\sigma_2 -c_{2,\sigma_1 ^\dag \sigma^z_{\sigma_1\sigma_2 c_{2,\sigma_2 ) + J\sum_{i=1,2,\mu S^\mu c_{i,\sigma}\sigma^\mu_{\sigma,\sigma' c_{i,\sigma' .}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-365: Two $c$ electrons couple to the $f$ local moment $S^\mu$ via a Kondo coupling term $(J>0)$.
elements.sent-2411-366: We can observe that $c$ electrons develop an AFM order due to the $m_z$ term which splits the energy of spin $\up$ and spin $\dn$ states of $c$ electrons at the same site.
elements.sent-2411-367: However, due to the existence of the $P\cdot\mathcal{T}$ symmetry, $c_{1,\up}$ and $c_{2,\dn}$ electrons are still degenerate (similarly, $c_{1,\dn}$ and $c_{2,\up}$ electrons are also degenerate).
elements.sent-2411-368: This degeneracy allows the formation of the Kondo singlet.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Filling-enforced metallic phase of the $c$ electrons
elements.sent-2411-372: Besides the Kramers double, the development of the Kondo effect also requires a finite density of states of $c$ electrons near the Fermi energy.
elements.sent-2411-373: In this section, we show that the $c$ electron band is always metal in the AFM phase.
elements.sent-2411-374: For the symmetry group (126.386) of the AFM phase, all the spinful representations at high symmetry points $A,M,R$ are four-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-375: Therefore, if the total fillings of the systems $n$ follow $n \text{ mod 4 \ne 0 $, then the system has to be metal.}
elements.sent-2411-376: In the current model, each Ce has $11$ electrons (without including the Ce $f$ electrons which provide the local moments), each Co has $9$ electrons, and each P has $5$ electrons.
elements.sent-2411-377: Then, for each unit cell, we have in total $11\times 2 + 9 \times 4 + 5 \times 4 = 78$ where $78 \text{ mod 4 =2 \ne 0 $.}
elements.sent-2411-379: The filling-enforced metallic nature of the $c$ electrons combined with the $P\cdot\mathcal{T}$ protected Kramers-doublet guarantee the development of the Kondo effect since we could always have $c$ electron density of states near the Fermi energy no matter how strong the magnetic order is.

elements.section-2411-011: Band topology of the Kondo phase
elements.sent-2411-437: We discuss the band topology of the anti-ferromagnetic Kondo phase in this Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-438: In this section, we will use the conventional cell of the PM phase which is the primitive cell of the AFM phase.
elements.sent-2411-439: We discuss the properties of glide mirror-$z$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-440: In this section, we consider the conventional unit cell.
elements.sent-2411-441: In the non-magnetic phase, the system exhibits mirror-$z$ symmetry with the mirror plane located at the Ce plane in~\cref{fig:lat_mag_structure , which is symmorphic.}
elements.sent-2411-442: However, in the magnetic phase, the mirror-$z$ symmetry maps Co$_{1,2}$ atoms to Co$_{3,4}$ atoms.
elements.sent-2411-443: Since Co$_{1,2}$ and Co$_{3,4}$ atoms possess opposite magnetic moments, mirror-$z$ is no longer a symmetry operation.
elements.sent-2411-444: However, by combining mirror-$z$ with translational symmetry, we obtain a glide mirror-$z$ transformation defined as \ba h = \{ M_z |1/2,1/2,1/2\} \ea which characterizes a mirror $z$ transformation followed by a $(1/2,1/2,1/2)$ shifting.
elements.sent-2411-445: It is worth noting that, in the non-magnetic phase, the $(1/2,1/2,1/2)$ translation is also a symmetry operation, meaning that there is no nonsymmorphic symmetry in this phase.
elements.sent-2411-446: The magnetic order breaks both the $(1/2,1/2,1/2)$ translational symmetry and the mirror-$z$ symmetry but preserves the glide mirror-$z$ symmetry ${M_z |1/2,1/2,1/2}$.

elements.sent-2411-618: In this section, we demonstrate that a relatively flat band that is atomic along $z$ direction and non-atomic along $x,y$ directions leads to a type-A antiferromagnetism (in-plane magnetism and out-of-plane antiferromagnetism).
elements.sent-2411-620: In this section, we will pick a toy model to demonstrate the ferromagnetism/antiferromagnetism tendency induced by flat bands.
elements.sent-2411-621: We will consider the case of a half-filled single flat band to simplify the considerations.
elements.sent-2411-622: We will ignore the contributions of the other dispersive bands.
elements.sent-2411-623: The flat band, when it appears near Fermi energy, induces a large density of states.
elements.sent-2411-624: We expect the leading-order instability to be generated by flat bands instead of dispersive bands.
elements.sent-2411-625: We comment that the real materials are more complicated, in which the flat bands are not perfectly flat and the dispersive bands could also make contributions.
elements.sent-2411-626: However, based on the toy model we considered in this appendix and the more realistic DFT model we discussed in Appendix~\ref{sec:app:afm_c , we could conclude that flat bands (atomic along $z$ direction and non-atomic along $xy$ direction) indeed favor the type-A antiferromagnetism.}

elements.sent-2411-742: In all these compounds, both magnetism and the Kondo effect originate from the $f$-electron states, and the magnetic transition temperature is comparable to or lower than the Kondo temperature.
elements.sent-2411-743: The coexistence is attributed to the distinct behaviors of $f$ electrons associated with different orbitals or different lattice sites\cite{PhysRevLett.123.106402,PhysRevB.106.125120,PhysRevB.107.L201104 .}
elements.sent-2411-744: In contrast, in CeCo$_2$P$_2$, the AFM order is formed by the $c$ electrons ($d$ orbitals of Co atoms), rather than the $f$ electrons, and the AFM ordering temperature is much higher than the Kondo temperature.
elements.sent-2411-745: These distinctions suggest a fundamentally different mechanism for the coexistence of Kondo screening and magnetism in CeCo$_2$P$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-746: The development of the Kondo effect in the AFM phase arises from the $P \cdot \mathcal{T}$ symmetry, where $P$ represents inversion and $\mathcal{T}$ denotes time-reversal transformation, and the filling-enforced metallic nature of the $c$ electrons.
elements.sent-2411-747: In contrast, our work provides new insights into the origin of antiferromagnetism, the coexistence of Kondo screening and magnetic order, and the topological properties of the system.

elements.sent-2411-773: Moreover, due to the presence of the $P \cdot \mathcal{T}$ symmetry, magnetic ordering of the $c$ electrons does not induce magnetic ordering of the $f$ electrons.
elements.sent-2411-774: This contrasts with the conventional expectation, where $c$-electron magnetism can induce $f$-electron magnetic order through a Hartree-Fock contribution.
elements.sent-2411-775: In our case, the site symmetry group of Ce atom has a $P \cdot \mathcal{T}$ symmetry with \begin{equation}P \cdot T \, J^\mu_{\RR, a} \, (P \cdot T)^{-1} = -J^\mu_{\RR, a},\end{equation} where $\bm{J ^{\mu _{\RR,a}$ denotes the total angular momentum of the $f$ electrons at site $\RR$ and sublattice $a \in \{\mathrm{Ce _1, \mathrm{Ce _2\}$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-777: In other words, the magnetic ordering of the $c$ electrons, which respects the $P \cdot \mathcal{T}$ symmetry, cannot induce a magnetic order in the $f$ electrons.
elements.sent-2411-778: This is also important for the formation of the Kondo effect, since the magnetic ordering of $f$ electrons will also suppress the Kondo effect.
elements.sent-2411-779: For the purpose of discussion, we also consider the case in which the $P \cdot \mathcal{T}$ symmetry is broken.

elements.sent-2411-782: In this section, we further investigate the hybridization function by comparing our system with the well-studied heavy-fermion compound CeCoIn$_5$.
elements.sent-2411-783: By analyzing the hybridization functions of CeCo$_2$P$_2$ and CeCoIn$_5$, we find that the relatively high Kondo temperatures observed in CeCo$_2$P$_2$ can be attributed to its stronger hybridization strength.
elements.sent-2411-784: Our focus is on the hybridization function associated with the low-energy $f$-electron state characterized by total angular momentum $J = 5/2$.
elements.sent-2411-785: For CeCo$_2$P$_2$, the corresponding hybridization function can be obtained as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta_{J=5/2}(\omega) = \frac{\pi}{N} \sum_{a, i=1,...,6}\delta_{a,Ce_1}\sum_{\kk,n}\bigg| \sum_{bj\sigma} V_{ai,bj\sigma}(\kk) U^c_{bj\sigma,n}(\kk) \bigg|^2 \delta(\epsilon-\epsilon_{\kk,n})\end{equation} Here, $V_{ai,bj\sigma (\kk)$ denotes the $fc$ hybridization, and $U^c_{bj\sigma,n (\kk)$ are the eigenvectors of the $c$-electron bands, as defined in \cref{eq:def_hyb_ham,eq:def_c_ham .}}}
elements.sent-2411-786: We consider only the contributions from the Ce$_1$ atom, since both Ce atoms are equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-787: Furthermore, we restrict the analysis to the lowest 6 $f$-electron states with total angular momentum $J = 5/2$.
elements.sent-2411-788: The equivalent hybridization function for the $J = 5/2$ $f$-orbitals in CeCoIn$_5$ can be defined in the same manner.
elements.sent-2411-789: In Fig.~\ref{fig:compare_hyb , we compare the hybridization functions and the density of states of the $c$ electrons, $\rho_c(\omega)$, for the two compounds (CeCo$_2$P$_2$ and CeCoIn$_5$).}
elements.sent-2411-790: The $c$-electron density of states is defined as \begin{equation}\rho_c(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\kk,n}\delta(\epsilon-\epsilon_{\kk,n})\end{equation} We conclude that the hybridization function of CeCo$_2$P$_2$ at the Fermi energy is much larger than that of CeCoIn$_5$, with the latter being approximately 30\% of the former.
elements.sent-2411-791: This enhanced hybridization can be attributed to the larger $c$-electron density of states in CeCo$_2$P$_2$, which allows more $c$ electrons to participate in the hybridization.
elements.sent-2411-792: Such enhanced hybridization is expected to result in a higher Kondo temperature, which is qualitatively consistent with experimental observations: the Kondo temperature of CeCo$_2$P$_2$ is estimated to be around 100K, much higher than that of CeCoIn$_5$, which is approximately 40K ~\cite{petrovic2001heavy,RevModPhys.92.011002 .}
elements.sent-2411-793: Finally, we comment that the lattice constants may vary with temperature, as reported in Ref.~\cite{reehuis1998antiferromagnetic , implying a potential temperature dependence of the hybridization functions.}
elements.sent-2411-794: Such variations could, in turn, influence the effective Kondo temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-795: Nonetheless, our results suggest that the relatively high Kondo temperature of CeCo$_2$P$_2$ may be understood via its relatively strong hybridization function.
elements.sent-2411-796: To further demonstrate the coexistence of the Kondo effect and antiferromagnetism (AFM), we perform embedded dynamical mean-field theory (eDMFT) calculations\cite{RevModPhys.78.865,RevModPhys.68.13,PhysRevLett.115.256402,PhysRevLett.115.196403 , combining DFT with DMFT.}
elements.sent-2411-797: The calculations are carried out in the AFM phase at $T = 100$ K, using lattice structures taken from the experimental results of Ref.~\cite{REEHUIS1990961 .}
elements.sent-2411-798: In Fig.\ref{fig:edmft_k_spec , we present the $k$-resolved interacting spectral function, where the $J=5/2$ $f$-electron states are observed near the Fermi energy.}
elements.sent-2411-799: Similar features are seen in the local density of states (DOS) shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:edmft_k_spec .}
elements.sent-2411-800: Both results indicate the development of the Kondo effect within the AFM phase.
elements.sent-2411-801: Our eDMFT results at $T=100$~K suggest that the Kondo temperature within the theoretical calculations is higher than 100~K.
elements.sent-2411-802: We note, however, that the lattice constants can vary with temperature in experiments, as reported in Ref.~\cite{reehuis1998antiferromagnetic .}
elements.sent-2411-803: Such variations, which are not captured in the present theoretical calculations, could also affect the Kondo temperature.
elements.sent-2411-804: Therefore, an accurate theoretical determination of the Kondo temperature would require accounting for the temperature dependence of the lattice structure, which would involve rather costly calculations.
elements.sent-2411-805: We thus leave this for future work.
elements.sent-2411-806: Based on our current calculations, we can conclude that: (1) the Kondo effect coexists with antiferromagnetism; and (2) the Kondo temperature is relatively high.
elements.sent-2411-807: This relatively large Kondo temperature can be understood in terms of the strong hybridization function discussed near Fig.~\ref{fig:compare_hyb .}
